title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
summary:
Mr. Cattermole questions Margaret Ernst regarding her refusal to allow him to interview Ambrose Flavard. Margaret explains that further questioning would only terrify the villagers, who are already struggling with a contagious sense of guilt regarding their past persecution of the Ives couple. This discussion follows Ambrose's observation that Jeanne's speech patterns had changed, as she began imitating the polite, well-educated tone used by Patrick and Frances Ives.

The conversation shifts to theology and morality. Margaret expresses resentment toward religious hypocrisy, specifically criticizing Ada Gransbury for blaming Patrick Ives for sins rather than following church tenets regarding judgment. Margaret argues that true love can transcend religious doctrine and suggests that hate, rather than love, is the true driver of the killings, though she maintains an intolerance for murder.

Following the meeting, Cattermole travels by train to London. He reflects on his decision to respect Margaret's wishes, believing that avoiding pressure may eventually lead people to reveal information voluntarily. He also contemplates his professional relationship with Hercule Poirot, noting that while Scotland Yard officially credits him, the true credit for investigations always goes to Poirot. During the journey, Cattermole has a vivid dream in which he is condemned for a crime he did not commit and sees his own gravestone marked with a cufflink engraved with his initials.

Meanwhile, Poirot and Inspector Stanley arrive at a villa in Belgravia to visit Nancy Duane. They are met by Nancy, a beautiful and fashionable woman of approximately forty years, who correctly anticipates the seriousness of their visit.
